text: From Joe Sobinakhe to Desmond Tutu, South Africa boasts a rich history of prophetic preaching, but in the new South Africa in which half of our nation is poor, the context to this style of preaching has to be understood. This article attempts to probe the conditions necessary for prophetic preaching in South Africa to take root again, arguing that there is the need for a homiletical understanding of poverty in the world we live in, an understanding of solidarity in the churches towards the poor, a homiletical analysis of the form and content of the different types of prophecy that could in a sense be seen as an independent genre of preaching. Support from church, denominations and the ecumenical movement is also crucial for an impactful prophetic ministry in a divided society like ours.
